en.extract:
The purple sunbird (Cinnyris asiaticus) is a small bird in the sunbird family. It occurs in parts of the Arabian peninsula and South and Southeast Asia. It has a fast and direct flight and can take nectar by hovering like a hummingbird but often perches at the base of flowers. It feeds mainly on nectar and insects, especially when feeding young. The males can appear all black in harsh sunlight but the purple iridescence is visible on closer observation or under good light conditions. Females are olive above and yellowish below.


Description
The purple sunbird has a relatively short bill, a dark and short square ended tail with distinctive sexual dimorphism. Less than 10 cm (3.9 in) long they have a down-curved bill with brush-tipped tubular tongues that aid in nectar feeding. The male is glossy metallic bluish to purplish  black on the upper parts with the wings appearing dark brown. The breeding male also has underparts of the same purplish black, but non-breeding males may show a central streak of black on yellow underparts. In the breeding plumage, the male can be confused with the syntopic Loten's sunbird which has a longer, more downcurved bill, lacks a supercilium, and has a distinctive broad maroon band on the breast. Breeding males will sometimes show their yellow pectoral tufts in displays. There is a patch of bright blue on the shoulder of breeding males. The maroon shine on the feathers of the collar around the neck is visible mainly during the breeding seasons.
Females are olive brown above with a yellowish underside. There is a pale supercilium beyond the eye. There is a darkish eye stripe. The throat and breast are yellow, becoming pale towards the vent. The outer tail feathers are tipped in white both in the male and female.
The young are just like females in plumage, however, the males get black feathers as they become adults. 
They are seen in pairs or small groups and aggregations may be found in gardens with suitable flowers. They feed mainly on nectar but also take fruits and insects. Groups of as many as 40 to 50 individuals have sometimes been noted.


Distribution and habitat
The species is distributed widely from West Asia through the Indian subcontinent and into Southeast Asia. They are resident birds in most parts of their range and do not move large distances. They are found in thin forest and garden land, including those in dense urban areas. Local movements are, however, noted especially in the drier parts of northwestern India and Pakistan where they are said to arrive in large numbers before summer.
The nominate subspecies is distributed in India east of the desert region and south of the Himalayas extending to the west and south of India and Sri Lanka. It mainly inhabits the plains but going up to 2,400 m (7,900 ft) in southern India and up to 1,700 m (5,600 ft) in the Himalayas. The race brevirostris is found in the dry zone from the Arabian Peninsula into Iran, Afghanistan, Pakistan until the dry zone of Rajasthan and Gujarat. These may, however, winter south near Goa. This form has a slight tinge of green in the pectoral yellow tufts. The race intermedius extends from the border of Orissa and Andhra Pradesh northwards into Bangladesh, Myanmar and Indochina.
The movements of the purple sunbird is not well understood. A male ringed in Bharatpur was recovered in Dehra Dun about 350 km (220 mi) to the north.


Behaviour and ecology

These birds are very vociferous and will call and will join to mob owls or other predators. The song is rapid rattle followed by ringing, metallic notes. Other call notes include a "chwit" or "chwing!" notes. The primary breeding season is before the Monsoons, April to June in northern India and January to June in Sri Lanka. While feeding they flick their wings. They rarely hover at flowers and usually perch to forage for nectar. They are important pollinators of some plant species such as Butea monosperma, Acacia, Woodfordia and Dendrophthoe. but they sometimes steal nectar by slitting flowers such as Hamelia patens at the base. They are known to feed on small berries such as those of Salvadora persica and cultivated grapes. Insects are sometimes caught by flycatching.

In courtship displays the male raises his head, fans his tail and flutters with partly open wings that expose the pectoral tufts and sings before the female. The nest is a pouch made of cobwebs, thin strips of vegetation, lichens and bark. The entrance hole on the side is often shaded by an overhanging projection. The nest is built almost entirely by the female. The nest material is not woven and most of it is held together by cobwebs. About five to ten days may be taken in the building of the nest. The inner cavity is expanded by the bird by opening its wing and turning around on the inside. In Sri Lanka and in southern India, it sometimes builds its nest by modifying and lining the cobweb structures formed by colonial or 'social' spiders, Stegodyphus sarasinorum (Eresidae). Two eggs are usually laid. The nest is usually suspended from a low branch, often of thorny plants but are sometimes built close to human habitations, attached to wires or other man-made objects and even indoors in an unused toilet. Only the female incubates the eggs which hatch after 15 to 17 days. Males assist in feeding the chicks although females involve themselves to a greater extent, making more trips as the chicks get older.
Sunbirds have been known to live for nearly 22 years in captivity.


References


External links

Purple sunbird videos, photos & sounds on the Internet Bird Collection.
Calls from Xeno-Canto

''Nectarinia mahrattensis'' }} The '''purple sunbird''' ('''''Cinnyris asiaticus''''') is a small [[bird]] in the [[sunbird]] family. It occurs in parts of the Arabian peninsula and South and Southeast Asia. It has a fast and direct flight and can take nectar by hovering like a hummingbird but often perches at the base of flowers. It feeds mainly on nectar and insects, especially when feeding young. The males can appear all black in harsh sunlight but the purple iridescence is visible on closer observation or under good light conditions. Females are olive above and yellowish below. ==Description== The purple sunbird has a relatively short bill, a dark and short square ended tail with distinctive sexual dimorphism. Less than {{cvt|10|cm}} long they have a down-curved bill with brush-tipped tubular tongues that aid in nectar feeding. The male is glossy metallic bluish to purplish{{Cite book|title=A Field Guide to the Birds of South-East Asia}} black on the upper parts with the wings appearing dark brown. The breeding male also has underparts of the same purplish black, but non-breeding males may show a central streak of black on yellow underparts. In the breeding [[plumage]], the male can be confused with the syntopic [[Loten's sunbird]] which has a longer, more downcurved bill, lacks a supercilium, and has a distinctive broad maroon band on the breast.{{Cite book |last=Grimmett |first=Richard |title=Birds of the Indian Subcontinent |last2=Inskipp |first2=Carol |last3=Inskipp |first3=Tim |publisher=Christopher Helm |year=2016 |isbn=978-81-93315-09-5 |edition=2nd |location=India |pages=454-455 |language=en}} Breeding males will sometimes show their yellow {{birdgloss|pectoral tufts}} in displays. There is a patch of bright blue on the shoulder of breeding males.{{cite book|author1=Rasmussen P.C. |author2=Anderton, J.C. |year=2005 |title=Birds of South Asia eries=The Ripley Guide |publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions |volume=2 |page=548}} The maroon shine on the feathers of the collar around the neck is visible mainly during the breeding seasons.{{cite journal|author=Ticehurst, C.B. |year=1918 |title= The plumage of the Purple Honeysucker ''Arachnecthra asiatica'' |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=26 |issue=1 |pages=286–287 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2049853}}{{cite journal|author=Dewar, Douglas |year=1906|title= The plumage of the cock Purple Honeysucker (''Arachnecthra asiatica'') A query? |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=17|issue=2 |pages=540–541 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30119736}}{{cite journal |author=Dewar, D. |year=1911|title=Indian Sunbirds |journal=Journal of the Foreign Bird Club |url=https://archive.org/stream/birdnotesns02fore#page/n174/mode/1up |pages=129–134}}{{cite journal |author=Finn, F. |year=1898 |title= Note on the seasonal change of plumage in the males of the Purple Honeysucker (''Arachnechthra asiatica'') and of an analogous American bird (''Coereba cyanea'') |journal=Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal |volume=67|issue=2 |pages=64–66 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/35583197}} Females are olive brown above with a yellowish underside. There is a pale [[supercilium]] beyond the eye. There is a darkish eye stripe. The throat and breast are yellow, becoming pale towards the vent. The outer tail feathers are tipped in white both in the male and female. The young are just like females in plumage, however, the males get black feathers as they become adults. They are seen in pairs or small groups and aggregations may be found in gardens with suitable flowers. They feed mainly on nectar but also take fruits and insects. Groups of as many as 40 to 50 individuals have sometimes been noted. ==Distribution and habitat== The species is distributed widely from [[West Asia]] through the [[Indian subcontinent]] and into [[Southeast Asia]]. They are resident birds in most parts of their range and do not move large distances. They are found in thin forest and garden land, including those in dense urban areas. Local movements are, however, noted especially in the drier parts of northwestern India and Pakistan where they are said to arrive in large numbers before summer.{{cite journal |title=Local bird-migration in India|author=Dewar, D. |pages=343–356 |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=18 |issue=2 |year=1908 |url=https://archive.org/stream/journalofbombayn18bomb#page/346/mode/2up}} The nominate [[subspecies]] is distributed in [[India]] east of the desert region and south of the [[Himalayas]] extending to the west and south of India and Sri Lanka. It mainly inhabits the plains but going up to {{cvt|2400|m}} in southern India and up to {{cvt|1700|m}} in the Himalayas. The race ''brevirostris'' is found in the dry zone from the Arabian Peninsula into Iran, Afghanistan, Pakistan until the dry zone of Rajasthan and Gujarat. These may, however, winter south near Goa.{{cite journal |title=Notes on the birds of the Londa neighbourhood, Bombay Presidency |author=Koelz, W. |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=43 |issue=1 |pages=11–38 |year=1942 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48227571}} This form has a slight tinge of green in the pectoral yellow tufts.{{cite book|author=Gadow, H. |title=Catalogue of the birds in the British Museum. Volume 9 |year=1884 |publisher=British Museum, Taylor & Francis |pages=56–60 |url=https://archive.org/stream/cataloguebirdsi03zoolgoog#page/n71/mode/1up}} The race ''intermedius'' extends from the border of Orissa and Andhra Pradesh{{cite journal|author=Majumdar, N. |year=1980|title= Occurrence of the Bengal Black Robin, ''Saxicoloides fulicata erythrura'' (Lesson) [Muscicapidae: Turdinae], and the Assam Purple Sunbird, ''Nectarinia asiatica intermedia'' (Hume) [Nectariniidae] in Orissa State |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=77 |issue=2 |page=334 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/44708256}} northwards into [[Bangladesh]], [[Myanmar]] and [[Indochina]]. The movements of the purple sunbird is not well understood. A male ringed in Bharatpur was recovered in Dehra Dun about {{cvt|350|km}} to the north.{{cite book|author1=Ali, S. |author2=Ripley, S.D. |name-list-style=amp |year=1999|title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan |volume=10 |edition=2nd |publisher=Oxford University Press |pages=34–39}} ==Behaviour and ecology== [[File:Purple Sunbird in Bhigwan August 2025 by Tisha Mukherjee 01.jpg|thumb|Purple Sunbird ([[species:Cinnyris asiaticus|''Cinnyris asiaticus'']]) male in non-breeding plumage feeding on [[Lantana camara]]]] These birds are very vociferous and will call and will join to mob owls or other predators. The song is rapid rattle followed by ringing, metallic notes. Other call notes include a "chwit" or "chwing!" notes. The primary breeding season is before the Monsoons, April to June in northern India and January to June in Sri Lanka. While feeding they flick their wings. They rarely hover at flowers and usually perch to forage for nectar. They are important pollinators of some plant species such as ''[[Butea monosperma]]'',{{cite journal|title=Reproductive Biology of ''Butea monosperma'' (Fabaceae)|journal=Annals of Botany|volume=92|pages=715–723 |year=2003 |doi=10.1093/aob/mcg193 |author1=Tandon, R. |author2=Shivanna, K.R. |author3=Mohan Ram, H.Y. |pmid=14500327 |pmc=4244857 |issue=5}} ''[[Acacia]]'',{{cite journal|title=Pollination ecology of acacias (Fabaceae, Mimosoideae) |journal=Australian Systematic Botany |volume=16|pages=103–118|author1=Stone, G.N.|author2=Raine, N.E. |author3=Prescott, M. |author4=Willmer, P. G. |year=2003 |doi=10.1071/SB02024 |url=http://www.biology.qmul.ac.uk/research/staff/chittka/chittkalab/PDF/Nigel/Stone_et_al_2003.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061014090459/http://www.biology.qmul.ac.uk/research/staff/chittka/chittkalab/PDF/Nigel/Stone_et_al_2003.pdf|archive-date=2006-10-14}} ''[[Woodfordia]]''{{cite journal |title=Passerine bird pollination and seed dispersal in ''Woodfordia floribunda'' Salisb. (Lythraceae), a common low altitude woody shrub in the Eastern Ghats forests of India.|author=Raju, A.J.S. |journal=Ornithological Science |year=2005 |volume=4 |issue=2 | pages=103–108 |doi=10.2326/osj.4.103|s2cid=198150132|doi-access=free }}{{cite journal |title=Bird-pollination in ''Sterculia colorata'' Roxb. (Sterculiaceae), a rare tree species in the Eastern Ghats of Visakhapatnam and East Godavari Districts of Andhra Pradesh |journal= Current Science |volume=87|issue=1|year=2004 |author1=Raju, A.J.S. |author2=Rao, S.P. |author3=Ezradanam, V. |pages=28–31|url=http://www.ias.ac.in/currsci/jul102004/28.pdf}} and ''[[Dendrophthoe]]''.{{cite journal|author1=Rahman, M.M. |author2=Baksha, M.W. |author3=Sterringa, J.T. |year= 1993 |title=Ethological observations of the purple sunbird (''Nectarinia asiatica'' Latham): a mistletoe-frequenting bird |journal=Indian Forester |volume=119 |issue=5|pages=388–394}} but they sometimes [[nectar theft|steal nectar]] by slitting flowers such as ''[[Hamelia patens]]'' at the base.{{cite journal |author=Ede, J.A.M.|year=1945|title=Sunbirds and flowers.|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume= 45 |issue=2 |page=234|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48126932}} They are known to feed on small berries such as those of ''[[Salvadora persica]]''{{Cite journal |author=Hoffmann, T. W. |year=1993|title=Fruit eating by Sunbirds of the Genus ''Nectarinia'' in Sri Lanka |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=90|issue=2|pages=291–292 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48609546}} and cultivated grapes.{{cite journal|author1=Sharma, S. |author2=Kashyap, R.K. |year=1999 |title=Purple Sunbird ''Nectarinia asiatica'' (Latham) – a new pest of grapes under agroclimatic conditions of Hissar, Haryana |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume= 96 |issue=2 |page=322 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48583001}} Insects are sometimes caught by flycatching.{{cite journal|author=Nair, M. V. |year=1994 |title= Flycatching by Sunbirds ''Nectarinia asiatica'' (Latham) |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=91 |issue=3|page= 457|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48378940}} [[File:003 Purple sunbird in Jim Corbett National Park Photo by Giles Laurent.jpg|alt=A small bird with iridescent blue feathers perched on a branch with bright red flowers. |thumb|Purple sunbird perched on an Indian coral tree ([[Erythrina variegata]]) in [[Jim Corbett National Park]], India.]] In courtship displays the male raises his head, fans his tail and flutters with partly open wings that expose the pectoral tufts and sings before the female. The nest is a pouch made of cobwebs, thin strips of vegetation, lichens and bark. The entrance hole on the side is often shaded by an overhanging projection. The nest is built almost entirely by the female. The nest material is not woven and most of it is held together by cobwebs. About five to ten days may be taken in the building of the nest. The inner cavity is expanded by the bird by opening its wing and turning around on the inside.{{cite journal |author=George, J. |year=1958|title= Nest construction technique of the Purple Sunbird |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume= 55|issue=3|pages=420–428 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48068939}} In Sri Lanka and in southern India, it sometimes builds its nest by modifying and lining the cobweb structures formed by colonial or 'social' spiders, ''[[Stegodyphus sarasinorum]]'' ([[Eresidae]]).{{cite book |editor=George, J. |year=1994|title=Annotated checklist of the birds of Bangalore|publisher=Birdwatchers' Field Club of Bangalore}}{{cite magazine |title=The nesting habits of the purple sunbird |volume=3 |issue=6 |date=June 1963 |magazine=Newsletter for Birders |url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW3#page/n83/mode/1up|author=Singh, B. |page=3}} Two eggs are usually laid.{{cite book |title=The Birds of India |author=Jerdon, T.C. |pages=370–372 |url=https://archive.org/details/birdsofindiabein01jerd/page/370 |volume=1 |year=1862 |publisher=Military Orphan Press}} The nest is usually suspended from a low branch, often of thorny plants{{cite book|pages=268–270 |author=Whistler, H. |title=Popular Handbook of Indian Birds |edition= 4th|publisher=Gurney & Jackson |year=1949|url=https://archive.org/stream/popularhandbooko033226mbp#page/n315/mode/1up/}} but are sometimes built close to human habitations, attached to wires or other man-made objects{{cite journal|author=Shipp, W.E. |year=1922 |title=Curious nesting site chosen by the Purple Honey Sucker ''Arachnethra asiatica'' |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=28 |issue=4 |page=1136|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/52170798}}{{cite journal|author=Sangha, Harkirat S. |year=1999 |title= An unusual nesting site of ''Nectarinia asiatica'' |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=96 |issue=3 |page= 475 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48583161}} and even indoors in an unused toilet.{{cite journal|author1=Rahmani, Asad R. |author2=Sankaran, Ravi |year=1990|title=An unusual nesting site of the sunbird |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=87 |issue=1|pages=148–149|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48806840}}{{cite journal |author=Munidasa, K.G.H. |year=1969 |title=Unusual nesting site of the Purple Sunbird, ''Nectarinia asiatica'' (Latham) |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume= 66|issue=1|pages=169–171|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47961254}}{{cite journal |author=George, J. |year=1957|title= A Sunbird's unusual nesting site |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume= 54|issue=4|pages=943–944|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48067088}} Only the female incubates the eggs which hatch after 15 to 17 days. Males assist in feeding the chicks{{cite journal |journal=Podoces |year=2007 |volume=2 |issue=2 |pages=122–126 |title=Notes on Feeding and Breeding Habits of the Purple Sunbird ''Nectarinia asiatica'' (''Cinnyris asiaticus'') in Bandar Abbas, Hormozgan, Southern Iran |author1=Ghadirian, T. |author2=Qashqaei, A.T. |author3=Dadras, M. |url=http://www.plan4land.org/english/biodiversity/publication_and_articles/notes_on_feeding_and_breeding_habits_of_purple_sunbird_in_bandar_abbas/notes_on_feeding_and_breeding_habits_of_purple_sunbird_in_bandar_abbas.pdf}} although females involve themselves to a greater extent, making more trips as the chicks get older.{{cite journal |year=2010 |title=On the patterns of parental provisioning by the Purple Sunbird ''Nectarinia asiatica'' |journal=The Sunbird |volume=40 |issue=2 |pages=39–47 |author1=Sethi, V.K. |author2=Bhatt, D. |author3=Kumar, A.}} Sunbirds have been known to live for nearly 22 years in captivity.{{cite journal|author=Flower, S. S. |year=1938|title=Further Notes on the Duration of Life in Animals.-IV. Birds |journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London, Series A |volume=A108 |pages=195–235 |doi=10.1111/j.1469-7998.1938.tb07895.x |issue=2}} File:1J6A0552-Purple Sunbird.jpg File:Cinnyris asiaticus 233584215.jpg|Female File:Purple Sunbird Cinnyris asiaticus nest Amravati Maharashtra.JPG|Nest File:Purple_sunbird_IMG_4981.jpg|Male displaying yellow pectoral tufts ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Cinnyris asiaticus}} * [http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/purple-sunbird-cinnyris-asiaticus Purple sunbird videos, photos & sounds] on the Internet Bird Collection. * [http://www.xeno-canto.org/asia/species.php?query=Nectarinia+asiatica Calls from Xeno-Canto] {{Taxonbar|from=Q1797393}} [[Category:Cinnyris|purple sunbird]] [[Category:Birds of South Asia]] [[Category:Birds of Indochina]] [[Category:Birds of the Middle East]] [[Category:Birds of the Arabian Peninsula]] [[Category:Birds of Yunnan]] [[Category:Birds described in 1790|purple sunbird]]

zh.extract:
紫色蜜鸟（学名：Cinnyris asiaticus）又称紫色花蜜鸟或紫花蜜鸟，为太阳鸟科雙領花蜜鳥屬的鸟类。分布于阿拉伯、伊朗、阿富汗、印度、尼泊尔、锡金、孟加拉、中南半岛、斯里兰卡以及中国大陆的云南等地，多栖息于海拔约950米以下的常绿阔叶林或村寨附近的丛林间。该物种的模式产地在印度古爾岡。
紫色花蜜鸟体重约8.1克，翼长约55毫米，嘴峰长约20.3毫米，喙宽度约3.7毫米，喙厚度约3.2毫米，跗蹠长约14.6毫米，尾长约35.1毫米。紫色花蜜鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在半开放的灌木丛中，食性为杂食性。


亚种
紫色蜜鸟指名亚种（学名：Cinnyris asiaticus asiaticus）。分布於印度、斯里蘭卡與尼泊爾。
紫色蜜鸟中南亚种（学名：Cinnyris asiaticus intermedius）。分布于孟加拉、印度、中南半岛以及中国云南等地。该物种的模式产地在印度孟加拉东部的Tipperah。
紫色蜜鸟中東亚种（学名：Cinnyris asiaticus brevirostris）。分布於阿拉伯半島東部、伊朗、阿富汗、巴基斯坦和印度西部。


参考文献
